coredata.dc:title: Three dimensional carbogenic dots/TiO2 nanoheterojunctions with enhanced visible light-driven photocatalytic activity
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jincheng
Zhu, Wenyu
Yu, Shuyan
Yan, Xiaoli
coredata.dc:description: Functional carbogenic quantum dots (C-dots) were synthesized by a pyrolysis approach with strong visible light absorption and high photoluminescence. The novel three dimensional (3D) hierarchical C-dots-TiO2 (C-TiO2) heterojunctions were obtained by assembling the C-dots with end carboxylic acid groups on the surface of 3D TiO2 nanorod spheres, and confirmed by scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy analyses. The prepared C-dots show strong adsorption of rhodamine B (RhB) and very low activity in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation. The prepared 3D C-TiO2 composites show enhanced visible light absorption and higher surface area with the increase of C-dot ratios. The highest photocatalytic activity is obtained at the carbon weight percent of 3.6%, and the corresponding apparent first-order rate constant of 0.00616min−1 which is 1.8 times of that by the pure 3D TiO2 sphere. The mechanism shows that C-dots play an important role in the donation of excited electron to the conduction band of TiO2 under visible light irradiation.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of C-dots-TiO2 nanorod sphere (C-TiO2) composite
Synthesis of C-TiO2 nanorod sphere composite
Photocatalysis properties under visible light irradiation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of synthesized C-dots. (b–d) High-resolution TEM images of C-dots. (e) UV–vis absorption spectrum of C-dots. (f) PL spectra of C-dots excited by different wavelength. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a–c) SEM images of 3D TiO2 nanorod spheres. (d) SEM image of 8.5% C-TiO2 composite. (e, f) TEM image of 3D 8.5% C-TiO2 composite. (g) HRTEM of 8.5% C-TiO2 composite.
EDX and elemental mapping of C-TiO2 composite. (a) SEM image, (b) EDX energy spectrum, (c-e) EDX elemental maps of C, O, Ti. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
TGA curves of C-TiO2 composites with different ratios of carbon dots. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XRD patterns (a) and FTIR spectra (b) of 3D TiO2 nanorod sphere and 3D C-TiO2 composite. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS analysis of the C-dots and C-TiO2. The full-scale XPS spectrum (a), high resolution XPS spectra of C1s (b). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
UV–vis absorption spectra (a) and N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (b) of the 3D TiO2 nanorod sphere and 3D C-TiO2 composites. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of original RhB and RhB after 1h adsorption by 3D TiO2 and C-TiO2 composites. (b) RhB adsorption rate by 3D TiO2 and C-TiO2 composites (1=3D TiO2, 2=1.9% C-TiO2, 3=3.6% C-TiO2, 4=5.3%, 5=8.5% C-TiO2, 6=11.1% C-TiO2). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–vis spectroscopic changes of the RhB aqueous solution in the presence of 3.6% C-TiO2 composite. (b) The photocatalytic degradation of RhB by 3D TiO2 nanorod sphere and C-TiO2 composites with different quantity of C-dots. (c) Kinetic plots with 3D TiO2 nanorod sphere and C-TiO2 composites with different quantity of C-dots. (d) Recyclability of the photocatalytic decomposition of RhB by 3.6% C-TiO2 composite. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The PL spectra of C-TiO2 composite irradiated at different wavelengths. (a) λ irradiation =280nm, (b) λ irradiation =420nm. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The mechanical illustration of enhanced visible-light driven photocatalytic activity for 3D C-TiO2 composite. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Three dimensional carbogenic dots/TiO2 nanoheterojunctions with enhanced visible light-driven photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, 50 Nanyang Avenue, Singapore 639798, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Civil and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Science & Engineering, Huaqiao University, Xiamen 361021, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanyang Environment and Water Research Institute (NEWRI), Nanyang Technological University, 1 Cleantech Loop, CleanTech One, Singapore 637141, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanyang Environment and Water Research Institute (NEWRI)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, 50 Nanyang Avenue, Singapore 639798, Singapore. Fax: +65 67909734.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Functional carbogenic quantum dots (C-dots) were synthesized by a pyrolysis approach with strong visible light absorption and high photoluminescence. The novel three dimensional (3D) hierarchical C-dots-TiO2 (C-TiO2) heterojunctions were obtained by assembling the C-dots with end carboxylic acid groups on the surface of 3D TiO2 nanorod spheres, and confirmed by scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy analyses. The prepared C-dots show strong adsorption of rhodamine B (RhB) and very low activity in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation. The prepared 3D C-TiO2 composites show enhanced visible light absorption and higher surface area with the increase of C-dot ratios. The highest photocatalytic activity is obtained at the carbon weight percent of 3.6%, and the corresponding apparent first-order rate constant of 0.00616min−1 which is 1.8 times of that by the pure 3D TiO2 sphere. The mechanism shows that C-dots play an important role in the donation of excited electron to the conduction band of TiO2 under visible light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, photocatalytic oxidation process has attracted wide attention in the removal of contaminants including toxic organic pollutants [1]. Among all kinds of semiconductor photocatalysts, titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been proven to be the most suitable and promising semiconductor catalyst in heterogeneous photocatalysis for its high physical and chemical stability, nontoxicity, high photocatalytic activity, and low price [2]. However, their poor responses to visible light caused by its wide band gap, and the fast recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs have significantly hindered the application of pure TiO2 in photocatalysis [3]. The combination with the visible responsive materials to form the heterojunctions is an effective route to prevent the charge recombination and extend the light adsorption to visible light area [4]. To date, the noble metal nanomaterials [4] and many narrow band gap nanomaterials such CdS [5], Bi2S3 [6], V2O5 [7], Ag2O [8], WO3 [9], Bi2O3 [10], BiVO4 [11] are coupled with TiO2 nanomaterials to improve its photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation.

Compared with the conventional narrow band gap nanomaterials, carbogenic quantum dots (C-dots) are a kind of new oxygen-containing carbonaceous nanomaterials with the virtue of high chemical stability, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and easy functionalization [12,13]. C-dots have adjustable visible light absorption and strong photoluminescence due to a quantum effect along with different emissive traps on the C-dot surface [14]. Till now, C-dots can be synthesized by a few of synthesis approaches [15,16]. Among them, pyrolysis method is an easy thermal oxidation method to directly produce functionalized C-dots [17,18]. The C-dots sensitized TiO2 can effectively extent the visible light absorption and improve the visible light photocatalytic activity. Nevertheless, there are few reports to produce high-efficient TiO2-based visible light driven photocatalysts linked with the C-dots with high photoluminescence and visible light absorption [19–21].

In addition to the light adsorption, the photocatalytic activity is significantly affected by the size, shape, surface area, morphology, and the dimensionality of the catalysts [22,23]. Small size one dimensional TiO2 nanorods have large surface area and direct charge transfer path to prevent the recombination of separated electron and hole, which offers it higher photocatalytic activity than TiO2 nanoparticles [24,25]. However, the small size TiO2 nanorods are easy to be aggregated without the capping surfactant [26,27], and hard to be recovered, which decreases its photocatalytic activity and limits its application. The three-dimensional (3D) hierarchical nanoarchitecture assembly of one dimension (1D) TiO2 nanorods is highly desired to solve those mentioned problems. It was reported that the 3D hierarchical TiO2 nanorod sphere has high surface-to-volume ratio, high organic pollutant adsorption, and excellent incident light scattering within the structures [28,29]. After further coupling with C-dots, it is highly expected to obtain the recoverable C-dots-TiO2 nanorod sphere (C-TiO2) heterojunction with high visible light-driven photocatalytic activity. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that 3D C-TiO2 nanorod sphere composites are fabricated for the study of visible light-driven photocatalytic property. Moreover, the optimization of C-TiO2 heterojunction is extremely important for the preparation of the high-efficient visible light-driven photocatalysts.

Here, we report the synthesis of the 3D C-TiO2 heterojunction composite, and their improved visible light photocatalytic activity. The C-dots with the end functional groups of carboxylic acid were produced by a modified pyrolysis approach, and the 3D TiO2 nanorod spheres were synthesized by a two-phase hydrothermal method. The 3D C-TiO2 composite with different weight content of C-dots were obtained by a hydrothermal coupling process in ethanol, and their apparent visible light-driven photocatalytic activity were investigated in the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB). The influence of C-dot content on the photocatalytic activity of C-TiO2 composite and the photocatalytic mechanism were also carefully studied. Our work will be interesting and helpful for the better design of recoverable visible light-driven photocatalysts.

Citric acid (CA, 99.5%), tert-butoxide titanium (99%, TBT), 6-aminohexanoic acid (AHA, 99%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99%) and concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.5%) were purchased from Alfa Aesar Chemical Company. Rhodamine B (RhB, 99%) and oleic acid (OLA, 90%) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Chemical Company. All solvents were purchased from Merck Ltd. The deionized (DI) water was produced from Millipore Milli-Q water purification system.

C-dots were synthesized by a pyrolysis approach modified from the earlier work [30,31]. Typically, 2g of AHA and 500mg of NaOH were mixed in 5mL of DI water. After stirring for 1h, the mixed aqueous solution was vacuum-dried for 12h to get the solid of 6-aminohexanoic sodium. The prepared 6-aminohexanoic sodium was washed by absolute ethanol twice to get rid of the residual sodium hydroxide. Then the purified 6-aminohexanoic sodium and 1g of citric acid were mixed in 5mL of DI water, and kept stirring for 2h. The ammonium salt of CA-AHA Na was formed and deposited by the addition of extra isopropanol, and vacuum dried for 12h. The dried CA-AHA Na ammonium salt was then sintered at 300°C for 2h. The sintered brown solid was dissolved in 5mL of water and deposited with large quantity of acetone to remove extra organic residuals. The purified C-dots as sodium salt were dispersed in the DI water again, and subsequently acidized by 2mL of 36.5% HCl. The final C-dots with the end group of carboxylic acid were redispersed in ethanol with the concentration of 5mgmL−1 for the further application.

Three dimensional TiO2 nanorod spheres were synthesized by a hydrothermal method [32]. Briefly, 2mL of TBT was mixed with 1mL of 36.5% HCl, and the mixture was slowly added into 10mL of oleic acid in a 45mL autoclave under stirring for 15min. At last, the autoclave was put in an electronic oven under 180°C for 4h. The prepared 3D TiO2 nanorod spheres were purified with acetone and ethanol several times before vacuum drying for 2h. The final white 3D TiO2 powders were stored for the further applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C-dots-TiO2 nanorod sphere (C-TiO2) composite
ce:section.ce:para: 100mg of 3D TiO2 and a certain volume of C-dots dispersions (2, 5, 7.5, 15, and 25mL) were mixed with absolute ethanol in a centrifuge tube with the final volume of 30mL. After sonication for 10min, the mixture was loaded into a 45mL autoclave for further solvothermal reaction at 90°C for 2h, and then centrifuged and purified with ethanol twice. The final orange C-TiO2 composite was vacuum dried for further characterization and photocatalytic experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained using a JEOL 2010-H microscope TEM operating at 200kV. The samples for the analysis were prepared by dropping C-TiO2 dispersion in ethanol onto 400-mesh carbon-coated copper grids and leaving the solvent to dry. The morphology and Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) of TiO2 nanorod sphere and C-TiO2 composite were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JEOL JSM-7600F). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed under dry air using a Perkin Elmer Thermogravimetric Analyzer TGA 7 with Thermal Analysis Controller TAC 7/DX. The samples were heated from room temperature to 800°C at a rate of 10°C/min. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were recorded in the range of 4000–400cm−1 on a Perkin–Elmer GX FT-IR system using compressed KBr disc technique. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were taken on a D8-Advance Bruker-AXS diffractometer using Cu Kα irradiation. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by using an Evolution 300 spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted by using a Kratos Axis Ultra Spectrometer with a monochromic Al Ka source at 1486.7eV, with a voltage of 15kV and an emission current of 10mA. Pore volume and size distributions were derived from the desorption branches of the isotherms by the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) model, and the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) equation was applied to calculate the specific surface area from the adsorption data.

The degradation of RhB dyes under visible light irradiation (λ >420nm) was tested by a BL-GHX-X photochemical reactor system irradiated by a 500W Xe lamp. The UV light below 420nm was filtered by the 1molL−1 NaNO2 solution, and the distance between the light source and the solution surface was 15cm. In a typical procedure, aqueous solution of the RhB dyes (1mL of 0.010mgmL−1 solution) and the photocatalysts (C-TiO2 composite) of 50mg dispersed in 49mL of DI water were placed into quartz capsule of 120mL. After stirring for 1h in the dark to reach the adsorption equilibrium, the photocatalytic reaction was started by turning on the lamp. After given time intervals, the 3mL photo-reacted solution was analyzed by recording variations of the absorption band maximum in the UV–vis spectra of RhB.

The C-dots were synthesized through a pyrolysis thermal oxidation method. After carefully getting rid of the deposition and some other impurities, the as-prepared C-dots can be dispersed in water for a long time. However, with the addition of 1mL HCl (35%), the C-dots become insoluble in water and well dispersed in ethanol. Fig. 1 shows the TEM images of prepared C-dots. It can be seen that the size of C-dots are from 6 to 20nm without clear aggregations (Fig. 1a). The high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 1b reveals the high crystallinity of synthesized C-dots with two different lattice spacings of 0.21 and 0.32nm (Fig. 1c and d), which are resulted from the in-plane lattice spacing of graphene (100facet) and the spacing between graphene layers (002facet) [33,34]. We also tried to synthesize C-dots with different mole ratios of AHA/CA. Nevertheless, with the ratio of 1:1, the C-dots are mostly aggregated (Fig. S1a). When too much AHA is used and the ratio of AHA/CA reaches 6:1, the size of some C-dots become very big (Fig. S1b). This suggests that the ratio of AHA/CA plays an important role in the synthesis of high quality C-dots.

The optical properties of C-dots were investigated by the UV–vis absorption spectra and PL spectra test. As shown in Fig. 1e, the C-dots present very strong visible light absorption at 300–500nm and a peak at 250nm, which are caused by the n–π∗ transition of CO groups and the π–π∗ transition of aromatic CC bonds [35,36]. In Fig. 1f, the C-dots show weak PL peak at about 509nm without evident shift excited by the light wavelength from 280 to 380nm, and the PL peak have a slight red-shift with the increase of excitation wavelength from 420 to 480nm. This result is in consistent with the earlier results from Fang et al. [37] and Yu et al. [38]. The highest PL intensity centered at 520nm with strong yellow fluorescence was obtained with the excitation wavelength of 420nm. The strong adsorption and fluorescence in the visible light region make the C-dots very good sensitizers to extend the light absorption of nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C-TiO2 nanorod sphere composite
ce:section.ce:para: The three dimensional TiO2 nanorod spheres were used to prepare the C-TiO2 composite. As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, the TiO2 sphere has a 3D spherical structure in diameter of about 4μm. The enlarged SEM picture shows the 3D TiO2 sphere is composed of numerous nanorods with the diameter of around 40nm. The smaller diameter of nanorods will offer the 3D TiO2 nanorod sphere larger surface area, and the one dimensional structure can effectively prevent the electron and hole recombination during photocatalysis [39]. Moreover, the 3D microsphere structure will avoid the aggregation problem of small size nanorods, overcome the bottleneck of individual 1D nanomaterials, and further improve the recycling feasibility of nanomaterials. Therefore, it is regarded as a very good candidate to prepare C-TiO2 composites with high visible light-driven photocatalytic activity.

After anchored by C-dots, the size and structure of TiO2 nanorod sphere have no obvious change (Fig. 2d).While it is difficult to observe clear nanorod structure in the SEM image because it is uniformly covered by C-dots. The TEM images display clearly that C-dots with the size of 10–20nm are anchored among TiO2 nanorods (Fig. 2e–g). The SEM and TEM analyses confirm the formation of C-TiO2 nanorod sphere heterostructure.

The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy was used to identify the elemental composition and ratios. As shown in Fig. 3 , the C-TiO2 composites are composed of C, O, and Ti elements with uniform distribution. Because the EDX analysis is not suitable used as quantitative analysis method, we further did the TGA analysis to confirm the weight ratios of carbon dots and TiO2 nanorod sphere. Fig. 4 shows the TGA curves of all the prepared C-TiO2 composites. The weight loss from 100 to 450°C are ascribed the decomposition of C-dots [40], and the residual solid is the TiO2 nanorod spheres. As shown in the Fig. 4, with the added C-dot volume of 2mL, 5mL, 7.5mL, 15mL and 25ml, the weight ratios of C-dots/TiO2 in the related C-TiO2 composite are 1.9%, 3.6%, 5.3%, 8.5% and 11.1%, respectively. This indicates that the carbon weight content in the C-TiO2 composite is controllable by the dosage of C-dots.

The crystalline structures of TiO2 nanorod sphere and its C-dots-coupled composites were investigated by the XRD patterns. As shown in Fig. 5 a, all the samples show very clear weak diffraction peaks at 25.3° resulted from the anatase TiO2 (JCPDS No. 21-1272), and strong diffraction peaks at 27.5°, 42.3° and 51.4° from the rutile TiO2 phase (JCPDS No. 21-1276). According to the calculation from the peak areas, the synthesized TiO2 nanorod sphere is composed of 94% rutile phase and 6% anatase phase. The mixed rutile and anatase phases can effectively separate the electrons and holes, and thus improve the photocatalytic activity [41]. After combined with different quantity of C-dots, no apparent change is observed in the XRD patterns of TiO2. In addition, due to the small quantity of C-dots, the broad weak diffraction peaks of C-dots at 18° are not observed [42]. The XRD results suggest that the combination of TiO2 and C-dots doesn’t change the crystalline structure and size of TiO2, which is in accordance with our TEM and SEM observations.

The chemical interactions between TiO2 and C-dots are interesting and important for the stability and properties of C-TiO2 composites. The FTIR spectra of C-dots, TiO2, and C-TiO2 composites are shown in Fig. 5b. It can be clearly seen that the FTIR spectrum of C-dots has very strong peaks from –COOH groups at 1720cm−1 [43]. Both TiO2 and C-TiO2 samples show the broad absorption bands below 1000cm−1, which are attributed to the combination of the Ti–O–Ti vibrations [44]. Moreover, the vibration band for C-TiO2 shows a slight red-shift compared to that of TiO2, which is caused by the combination of C-dots and the vibration of Ti–O–C vibration [45]. A weak peak at 1405cm−1 and a weak shoulder at 1439cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of 11.1% C-TiO2 is assigned to the coordination between carboxylic acid group and Ti center [25]. The FTIR result confirms that the C-dots are coordinated with TiO2 nanorod sphere by the end carboxylic acid groups.

The XPS analysis was used to further illustrate the interaction between C-dots and TiO2 nanorod sphere. As seen in Fig. 6 a, the full-scale XPS spectra show graphitic C1s peak at 284.8eV, O1s peak at 532eV and Ti2p peak at 458.5eV for 3D C-TiO2 composite [46], and the existence of C and O in the C-dots. In the high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 6b), three main separated peaks at 284.9, 286.5, and 289.2eV are existed for the C-TiO2 composite and C-dots, which are connected to the graphitic C–C, C–O and COOH bonds [47,48]. The ratios of OCO and C–O bonds in the C-dots of C-TiO2 composite are slightly lower than those of pure C-dots, which is caused by the loss during the hydrothermal coupling process. The XPS results confirm the existence of C-dots in the C-TiO2 composite, which is in good accordance with our SEM, TEM, TGA and FTIR analyses.

The extension of light adsorption range is very important for the photocatalytic activity of TiO2-based nanomaterials under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 7 a, TiO2 nanorod sphere has an absorption onset at 390nm, and no absorption in the visible light range. After coupling with 3.6% C-dots, the absorption band of C-TiO2 composite shows evident red-shift to the visible light region. With the 11.1% of C-dots, the C-TiO2 composite exhibits very strong visible light absorption from 400 to 800nm. This suggests that the addition of C-dots effectively increases the adsorption of TiO2 nanorod sphere in the visible light region, which is in good agreement with the reports in the literatures [20,21].

Aside from the light adsorption, the surface area is also critical to the photocatalytic activity. The BET of TiO2 nanorod sphere and C-TiO2 composite were tested by the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms. As shown in Fig. 7b, the isotherms of both TiO2 and C-TiO2 composite are connected to the type IV with H2 hysteresis loop, reflecting the presence of the porous structure [49]. The calculated BET results for the TiO2, 3.6% C-TiO2 and 11.1% C-TiO2 are 37.35, 58.94 and 87.88cm3 g−1. It is clear that the surface area of C-TiO2 composite increases with the increased quantity of C-dots on the surface of TiO2 nanorod sphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalysis properties under visible light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: As the 3D C-TiO2 composite has stronger visible light absorption and larger surface area than those of 3D TiO2 nanorod sphere, it is highly expected that the 3D C-TiO2 composite has higher photocatalytic activity than that of 3D TiO2 nanorod sphere. The photocatalytic activity of 3D TiO2 and C-TiO2 with different carbon weight percent are evaluated by the photodegradation of 0.5mgmL−1 RhB by the 1mgmL−1 photocatalysts under visible light irradiation (λ >420nm). Before starting the photocatalytic reaction, the mixture of RhB and photocatalyst are stirring for 1h to reach the adsorption equilibrium.

Fig. 8 a shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of RhB after adsorbed by photocatalysts. It is clear that the absorption peak intensity of RhB at 554nm undergoes a fairly decrease without evident shift after adsorbed by all the TiO2 and C-TiO2 photocatalysts. With the increase of carbon weight percent in the C-TiO2 composite, the peak intensity of RhB decrease gradually, which indicates more RhB is adsorbed by the photocatalysts before the photocatalytic degradation. The 11.1% C-TiO2 composite can adsorb 53% of RhB dye owing to its high surface area (Fig. 8b). The adsorption experiment indicates that the coupling of C-dots can significantly improve the adsorption of RhB.

During the photocatalytic degradation within 90min, the color of RhB slowly changes from pink to yellow (Fig. S2). The monitored UV–vis absorption spectra of RhB after photodegradated by the 3.6% C-TiO2 composite are shown in Fig. 9 a. Besides the gradual declined peak intensity, the maximum absorption band of the RhB solution gradually shifts from 554 to 499nm, which is caused by the N-deethylation of RhB during irradiation [50]. Fig. 9b shows the photocatalytic degradation dynamic curves of RhB by all the TiO2 and C-TiO2 photocatalysts. The results show that 31% of RhB can be degradated by pure 3D TiO2 nanorod sphere after 90min of irradiation, which is mainly attributed to the dye-sensitized photodegradation of RhB. After coupling with 1.9%, 3.6% and 5.3% C-dots, the 35%, 44% and 36% of RhB are degradated after 90min of irradiation, which are higher than that of pure 3D TiO2. However, when the C-dots quantity are further increased to 8.5% and 11.1%, only 27% and 12% of RhB are degradated after irradiated for 90min, which is even lower than that by pure 3D TiO2 nanorod sphere.

Moreover, according to the photocatalytic degradation kinetic curves shown in Fig. 9c, the photocatalytic reactions apparently follow pseudo first-order kinetics in the studied concentration range. The photocatalytic reaction can be simply described by dC/dt=kC, where C is the concentration of RhB and k denotes the overall degradation rate constant. The k (min− 1) values for 3D TiO2, 1.9% C-TiO2, 3.6% C-TiO2, 5.3% C-TiO2, 8.5% C-TiO2 and 11.1% C-TiO2 are estimated to be 0.00342, 0.0049, 0.00616, 0.005, 0.00125, and 0.000304min−1. From the analysis of photocatalytic kinetic constants, the photocatalytic activity of C-TiO2 is found to increase before the carbon weight percent reaches 3.6% and decrease gradually afterwards, proposing that the optimum coupled quantity of C-dots is 3.6% in our experiment. This is well consistent with the earlier results [20,21]. It is noteworthy that 3D TiO2 nanorod sphere as support material results in a higher value of 3.6% for the best C-dot coupling amount on 3D TiO2 than that of 1.5% on the TiO2 sphere in the earlier work [20,21]. For investigating the long-term stability of C-TiO2 composite, the photocatalytic degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation was performed during four cycles. As shown in Fig. 9d, no evident change has taken place for the photocatalytic activity during the 4 recycling degradation of RhB, which indicates good photocatalytic stability of the C-TiO2 composites.

From the earlier report, the conduction band (CB) of C-dots is from −0.15eV to −0.85eV depending on the surface state [51], and the CB of rutile TiO2 at −0.37eV [52]. It is possible for the electron transfer from the CB of C-dots to the CB of TiO2 or converse. The PL emission spectra of C-TiO2 composites were investigated to confirm the electron transfer. As shown in Fig. 10 , the PL spectrum of TiO2 nanorod spheres presents two main peaks at 425 and 506nm, which can be assigned to the direct bandgap transition and the oxygen vacancy with one trapped electron of chloride, respectively [53,54]. After anchoring the C-dots, a significant quenching of PL is observed, which shows 76% and 52% of the PL intensity of TiO2 nanorod sphere for the 3.6% C-TiO2 and 11.1% C-TiO2 composites, respectively. This indicates that effective charge transfer from TiO2 to C-dots takes place under UV light irradiation. However, the PL peak from C-dots is not observed for all the C-TiO2 composites irradiated by the lights of 280nm. Because C-dots show highest PL intensity under the irradiation light of 420nm, we also tested the PL spectra of C-TiO2 composite under this irradiation wavelength. However, the PL of C-dots is still not detected, which may be totally quenched by the connection with TiO2 nanorod sphere. The PL test confirms the electron transfer from the CB of TiO2 to C-dots under UV light irradiation. When irradiated under visible light, C-dots can work as a photosensitizer, and the excited electron can transfer from the CB of C-dots to that of TiO2, which is in good accordance with the earlier reports [20,21].

The photocatalytic activity is generally influenced by the adsorption of pollutants, the light adsorption, the charge separation and recombination, and adsorbed oxygen [55,56]. A small amount of C-dots on the surface of TiO2 can improve the dye adsorption, light adsorption and act as an effective donor, which leads to an improved photocatalytic activity. Whereas, excessive anchored C-dots will occupy the main photocatalytic active sites in the surface of TiO2 and hinder the surface O2 adsorption as well. Consequently, the excited electron injected to the CB of TiO2 with thick layer of C-dots can’t further react with O2, which leads to a high electron–hole recombination and a very poor photocatalytic activity even lower than that of pure 3D TiO2. In the work from Hu [57], the C-dots can effectively degradate methylene blue under visible light irradiation. However, our synthesized C-dots show strong adsorption of RhB but very poor photocatalytic activity on the degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation (Fig. S3), which may be caused by the different surface status and size [58]. This interprets the reason of the very low photocatalytic activity in the degradation of RhB by 11.1% C-TiO2 composite.

Based on the above results and discussion, a possible photocatalytic mechanism of the charge transfer and enhanced photocatalytic activity by C-TiO2 composites is summarized in Fig. 11 . Under visible light irradiation, the p conjugated C-dots act as a photosensitizer like organic dyes to sensitize 3D TiO2 sphere into a visible light responsive “dyade” structure, and donate the electrons to the conduction band of TiO2. The electrons in the CB of TiO2 react with O2 to form O2 −, and then oxidize the RhB dye. Meanwhile, the hole in the surface of C-dots with the standard electrode potential of 3.2eV will oxidize H2O/OH to form
OH radical (2.8eV), which also contribute to the oxidization of the RhB dye [59,60]. The more detailed theoretical calculation of band gap and the analysis of generated radicals will be done in the future works.

C-dots prepared by a simple pyrolysis method have strong visible light absorption and high photoluminescence. The 3D hierarchical C-TiO2 heterojunctions with different contents of C-dots were obtained by assembling the C-dots with end carboxylic groups with 3D TiO2 nanorod spheres, and confirmed by SEM, EDX, TEM, TGA and XPS analyses. With the increased ratios of C-dots, the prepared 3D C-TiO2 composites show an improved visible light absorption and larger surface area than that of pure 3D TiO2 nanorod sphere. The prepared C-dots show poor photocatalytic activity in the degradation of RhB, but can strongly adsorb the dye of RhB. The 3D C-TiO2 heterojunctions have the best photocatalytic activity at the carbon weight percent of 3.6%, and the corresponding apparent first-order rate constant of 0.00616min−1 which is 1.8 times of that by the pure 3D TiO2 microsphere. The mechanism shows that C-dots play an important role in the injection of excited electron to the conduction band of rutile TiO2 under visible light irradiation. Our work is interesting and helpful for the better design of carbon-TiO2 composite for a high effect photocatalytic activity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to acknowledge the Nanyang start-up Grants (SUG: M4081044) support for this work. Authors also are grateful to Dr. Athanasios, B. Bourlinos very much for the kind help and discussion in the synthesis of C-dots.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2014.07.079.
